file_name: Adablanu et al. - 2026 - Transforming skin cancer detection with AI‐based convolutional and transformer models.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a hybrid deep learning model called VINCE-NET, which integrates Vision Transformers (ViT), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, to classify melanoma using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and describes an experimental study where the authors adapted a hybrid deep learning model (VINCE-NET) to classify melanoma using the HAM10000 dataset. It presents original methods, training procedures, and quantitative results (e.g., 94.1% accuracy) comparing the proposed model against baselines, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Transforming Skin Cancer Detection With AI‐Based Convolutional and Transformer Models
brief_summary: This study adapts VINCE-NET, a hybrid deep learning model integrating Vision Transformers, Convolutional Neural Networks, and Long Short-Term Memory networks, for melanoma classification using dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset. The model was trained on a CPU-only environment with specific preprocessing steps including Gaussian blur and augmentation to handle class imbalance. Performance was evaluated against baseline models such as ResNet-50, VGG-16/19, and DenseNet-121/201 using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC. VINCE-NET achieved 94.1% accuracy, 95.5% precision, 90.4% recall, and an AUC-ROC of 0.98, outperforming the benchmark models. The results demonstrate that VINCE-NET provides competitive performance for melanoma detection while remaining computationally efficient for resource-limited settings. However, the authors note that the model has not yet been clinically validated and requires future external validation.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target for binary classification (malignant class) using the HAM10000 dataset.

Grouped into a 'benign' class for binary classification. The HAM10000 dataset comprises 7 classes including melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and benign keratosis, which were aggregated into benign vs. malignant groups for the experiment.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into benign and malignant classes (melanoma detection).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the study adapts VINCE-NET to 'classify melanoma' and performs 'binary classification' where images are grouped into benign and malignant classes. The model uses a sigmoid activation for binary classification and evaluates performance using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10,000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset comprising 10,015 dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions sourced from multiple populations. It is described as a standard benchmark for algorithmic evaluation in dermatology.

use.training: Used for training the VINCE-NET model and baseline models (CNN, LSTM, ResNet-50, VGG-16/19, DenseNet-121/201) with a stratified 70% split.
use.validation: Used for validation with a stratified 20% split.
use.testing: Used for testing with a stratified 10% split.
use.other: Used as the primary benchmark dataset for comparative performance evaluation against other studies listed in Table 3.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the HAM10000 dataset into training (70%), validation (20%), and testing (10%) subsets while preserving class distribution.
To mitigate class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset during model training.
To evaluate class-wise predictions, compute per-class metrics (precision, recall, specificity, F1-score), and analyze misclassification trends.
To assess the model's discrimination capability and calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC-ROC) for binary classification performance.

To compare VINCE-NET's accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC against baseline models (CNN, LSTM, ResNet-50, VGG-16/19, DenseNet-121/201) and existing literature models under identical or comparable conditions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between VINCE-NET and baseline models were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC-ROC) without statistical significance testing.

VINCE-NET (Proposed Model).notes: Results reported in Abstract and Section 3.1. Specificity and G-mean reported in Section 3.2. Training time reported in Abstract and Section 3.2.

VINCE-NET (Per-Class Metrics).notes: Table 2 reports per-class metrics. Specificity of one class corresponds to recall of the opposite class in this binary setting.

In-House Baselines (Identical Pipeline).notes: Table 4 reports in-house baselines under identical pipeline conditions (Google Colab T4 high-RAM, stratified 70/20/10 split). VINCE-NET had the highest F1-score and markedly shorter training time than several transfer-learning baselines.

Saha et al., 2024 (YOLOv8x-cls variant).notes: Preprocessing: HSV hue/saturation/value adjustments, translation, scaling, flips, shear, mosaic, RandAugment, random erasing. Split ratio: Not stated.

Setiawan and Soewito, 2025 (CRCDKD).notes: Preprocessing: 5-fold cross-validation, images resized to 224x224, normalized, augmented with random affine transforms and horizontal flips. Split: 5-Fold cross-validation (~80% training, 20% testing per fold).

Wicaksana et al., 2025 (YOLOv11x-cls).notes: Preprocessing: Segmentation using binary masks, cropping, resizing (640x640), rotation (±15°), flips. Split: 70% training, 20% validation, 10% testing.

Wicaksana et al., 2025 (VGG-19).notes: Preprocessing: Segmentation using binary masks, cropping, resizing (224x224), rotation (±15°), flips. Split: 70% training, 20% validation, 10% testing.

Said et al., 2022 (MobileNet).notes: Preprocessing: Data augmentation with random image crops, rotations, zooms, horizontal/vertical flips; ImageNet transfer learning. Split: 80% training, 20% validation.

Polat and Koc, 2020 (CNN + one-versus-al.notes: Preprocessing: No preprocessing. Split: 75% training, 5% verification, 20% testing.

Sahithi et al., 2025 (ViT-B/16).notes: Preprocessing: ViT base fine-tuned, images resized to 224x224, normalized. Split: Not stated.

Ozdemir and Pacal, 2025 (ConvNeXtV2 + se.notes: Preprocessing: Data augmentation (rotation, flipping, scaling, smoothing, mixing-up, color jittering). Split: 70% training, 15% validation, 15% testing.

architecture_used.main_model: VINCE-NET (hybrid deep learning model integrating Vision Transformers (ViT), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks)
architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported (model adapted from stroke detection; no specific ImageNet or other pretraining weights mentioned for the hybrid architecture itself)

LSTM_branch.input_type: Flattened CNN features treated as pseudo-sequences

training_details.loss_function: Class-weighted loss (binary cross-entropy implied by sigmoid output and class weighting)
training_details.hardware: CPU-only Google Colab (12.72 GB RAM, 107.7 GB disk)

preprocessing_techniques: Gaussian blur filtering (kernel size 5 × 5) for noise reduction and edge preservation
Color normalization (pixel intensities normalized to [0, 1] by dividing by 255.0)
Data augmentation including random rotation, flipping, shearing, zooming, and contrast enhancement

conclusions: VINCE-NET, a hybrid deep learning model integrating Vision Transformers, Convolutional Neural Networks, and Long Short-Term Memory networks, provides competitive performance for melanoma classification, achieving 94.1% accuracy, 95.5% precision, 90.4% recall, a 92.9% F1-score, and an AUC-ROC of 0.98 on the HAM10000 dataset.

VINCE-NET demonstrates feasibility and computational efficiency in resource-limited settings, as evidenced by its ability to train effectively on a CPU-only environment (Google Colab) with a training time of 34,308.42 seconds, outperforming baseline models in efficiency while maintaining high accuracy.
The model outperformed benchmark models including CNN, LSTM, ResNet-50, VGG-16/19, and DenseNet-121/201 under identical preprocessing and training conditions.
The study acknowledges that VINCE-NET has not yet been clinically validated.

The authors note limitations regarding the model's generalization, as the HAM10000 dataset is limited to a single source and binary classification, necessitating future external validation across diverse populations and imaging devices.
Future work is identified to address resolution trade-offs, ablation studies, model interpretability (incorporating SHAP, LIME, and attention visualizations), and external validation.
important_remarks: The model was trained on a CPU-only environment (Google Colab) rather than a GPU, which limits the scalability and real-world inference speed claims compared to standard deep learning benchmarks.

The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is limited to a single source and binary classification, raising concerns about generalization to diverse populations, imaging devices, and multiclass dermatological conditions.
VINCE-NET has not been clinically validated; its real-time deployment potential is currently theoretical and untested on mobile or embedded systems.
No formal inference latency measurements, runtime profiling, or deployment trials were conducted to support claims of computational efficiency in resource-limited settings.

The input image resolution was downscaled to 128 × 128 pixels for efficiency, which may limit the model's ability to capture subtle dermoscopic patterns such as fine pigment networks or micro-streaks.
The benefit of the LSTM branch for static dermoscopic images is unproven; ablation studies are needed to determine if it offers tangible performance gains over a ViT + CNN-only configuration.

Model interpretability was limited to Grad-CAM, which the authors acknowledge is insufficient for comprehensive explainability; future work plans to incorporate SHAP, LIME, and attention visualizations.
The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or statistical significance tests for the performance comparisons between VINCE-NET and baseline models.
The model was originally designed for stroke detection and adapted for melanoma classification without reported specific pretraining weights for the hybrid architecture on dermatological data.
Class-wise error analysis indicated slightly lower specificities for benign lesions compared to malignant lesions, likely due to visual similarity and inherent class imbalance in the dataset.
